text: The surgeon begins by incising the mesocolon at the junction of the right mesocolon and the small bowel mesentery. Next, the peritoneum anterior to the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) is incised along the midline.
The surgeon cuts the vascular sheath, revealing a clear space between the sheath and the artery. The operation proceeds safely in this space, as there are no small vessels crossing it.

The surgeon isolates the artery and vessel, carefully turning the working shell of the harmonic scalpel outward to prevent vessel damage. The surgical field is expanded, revealing the ileocolic vein and vessel. The central lymph node is dissected en bloc with the mesocolon, while small vessel branches from the superior mesenteric vein are visible.

The surgeon identifies and dissects lymph nodes numbered 221, 223, and 14, along with the mesocolon, in a block. The dissection occurs in the space anterior to the pancreas, with careful attention to avoid bleeding and injury to vessels and lymphatic tubes.
The surgeon traces and clips the branch of the vessel within the surgical field, where the central lymph node has been resected. The vessel appears smooth and the surgical space is expanded.

The surgeon cuts the omentum towards the spleen and hepatic flexure near the gastric block, dissecting the number C lymph node and cutting the transverse mesocolon at its root near the pancreas. The SMA lymph node is dissected, and the mesocolon is cut at its root towards the hepatic flexure. The space between the mesocolon and liver is visible, referred to as the tree junction. The hyperreflector is finished, and the tissue can be cut using the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon completes the right colon resection, revealing a smooth surgical field around the SMA, duodenal, and pancreatic head. A side-to-side conjunction is made, and glue is applied to block lymphatic vessels to prevent leakage. The specimen is displayed.
